name: Recruitment Pathway and Participant Flow
consequences: The structured pre-admission diagnostic pathway (specialist interview, collateral history, screening PSG, PI adjudication) filters for genuine NREM parasomnias and reduces safety exclusions, but tightens the eligible pool. Stricter intake gatekeeping improves data quality and regulatory standing; however, slower recruitment may necessitate extended timelines or protocol relaxation to meet the 50–70 participant target by month 36, risking pressure to admit borderline cases.
options: Maintain PI-led clinical gatekeeping with strict AASM-aligned exclusion criteria and accept that recruitment may extend beyond month 24, with contingency for protocol extension to month 42 if needed.

Delegate pre-screening to the clinical psychologist and trained study coordinator using a standardized decision tree, reserving PI review for borderline cases; this accelerates throughput but introduces consistency risk if the tree is incomplete.

Partner with the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin to establish a formal referral consortium across German sleep clinics, with regional specialists pre-screening candidates before University Hospital Bonn intake to increase flow.
review: This lever governs the trade-off between diagnostic rigor and recruitment velocity, directly affecting event-yield targets. The options overlook the cost and timeline impact of extending recruitment beyond the pilot phase, and do not address how to manage attrition if participant stays are delayed due to slow event capture.

name: Night-Shift Staffing and Event-Response Capacity
consequences: Rotating three technicians across 8 suites provides safe coverage with margin for event annotation and emergency response, but simultaneous multi-suite events or technician illness can create bottlenecks. Increasing technician count improves response reliability and annotation quality, but raises personnel costs (approximately €2M baseline over three years) and may exceed budget headroom without reducing other line items or extending the facility footprint.
options: Retain the three-technician roster with on-call backup from senior staff for simultaneous events or illness, relying on incident log-based debriefing and monthly coverage audits to identify strain points.

Hire a fourth full-time technician split 0.75 FTE night-shift and 0.25 FTE day-shift annotation support, accepting a €180K–220K annual cost increase offset by faster real-time annotation and reduced post-hoc review burden.

Implement a hybrid model: retain three core night technicians and contract part-time locum sleep technicians (€25–30/hour) on rotating on-call status, callable within 30 minutes for high-event-likelihood participants or multi-suite incidents.
review: This lever determines operational resilience and annotation throughput, critical to event-capture success and staff safety. The options do not address how to train or maintain consistency of locum technicians, or how to handle the coordination overhead of a part-time roster.

name: Sensing Architecture and Monitoring Burden
consequences: The tiered model (low-burden nightly dry-EEG and contact-free sensors, enhanced PSG on three scheduled nights plus escalation nights) captures high-resolution data during likely event windows while minimizing participant discomfort on routine nights. This balances naturalistic behavior preservation with physiological detail, but depends on accurate technician flagging for escalation nights—missed flags reduce clinical-grade event resolution and slow algorithm training.
options: Maintain the current three-night enhanced PSG schedule plus technician-flagged escalation, accepting that some low-salience or atypical events may be missed if not flagged in real time.

Expand scheduled enhanced PSG to five nights per stay (spread across the 2–8 week period) to increase probability of event capture during clinical-grade montage, accepting higher participant burden and equipment utilization; this may improve event-yield targets but increases costs (PSG supplies and technician time) by an estimated 10–15%.

Deploy continuous low-latency dry-EEG with local automated event-alert (threshold-crossing on chin EMG and movement-sensor metrics) to trigger technician review and optional PSG escalation only when high-confidence flags occur, reducing false escalations but requiring algorithm development before launch.
review: This lever balances data density against participant tolerance and cost. The options overlook the feasibility and validation timeline for automated alerting, and do not quantify how technician flagging accuracy affects event-yield or algorithm training data.

name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development and Validation Strategy
consequences: Building a semi-automated tool that ranks probable parasomnia episodes by human-scorer agreement (rather than claiming autonomous diagnosis) reduces manual review burden and enables rapid event screening, accelerating phenotyping analysis. However, algorithm performance depends directly on the quantity and quality of labeled training data—low event yield or inconsistent dual-scorer annotation degrades model utility, and any claimed accuracy beyond "human-agreement benchmarking" invites regulatory scrutiny.
options: Train the algorithm on consensus-labeled events from the core cohort (months 4–28) using standard supervised learning (random forest or logistic regression on engineered sensor features), reporting sensitivity and specificity against dual scorer agreement and reserving claims of independent diagnostic performance for phase-two work.

Collaborate with a computational neuroscience center (e.g., Max Planck Institute or Berlin Charité) to co-develop a more sophisticated deep-learning model using raw sensor streams, sharing code and intermediate models openly to accelerate publication and attract phase-two funding.

Develop a lightweight, interpretable rule-based triage system (decision tree on hand-crafted features like chin EMG amplitude, movement duration, and arousal timing) that does not require large labeled datasets and can be deployed earlier in the project timeline for pilot event screening.
review: This lever navigates the tension between methodological ambition and phase-one feasibility. The options do not address data annotation burden or inter-rater disagreement rates, which directly constrain training-data quality and may require protocol refinement mid-project.

name: Expansion and Facility Scaling Timeline
consequences: Deferring expansion from 8 to 12 suites until month 12–18 (after pilot validation) reduces upfront renovation and staffing costs, preserving capital for contingencies and ensuring operational stability before adding complexity. Delaying expansion limits cumulative participant enrollment (approximately 15–20 in year one vs. potential 25–30 with 12 suites) and may push the 50–70 target into year four unless recruitment accelerates in years two and three.
options: Proceed with 8-suite operation through month 24, then expand to 12 suites only if the external advisory board confirms safe operations, acceptable data quality, and recruitment velocity ≥8 participants per month, gated by available budget and staffing.

Conduct a mid-project facility assessment at month 12; if data quality and event-yield targets are on track, begin renovation of the additional 4 suites in parallel with ongoing operations, accepting temporary coordination overhead but accelerating enrollment ramp.

Plan a phased expansion: add 2 suites at month 9 and 2 more at month 15, distributing renovation cost and allowing incremental staffing adjustment; this requires careful scheduling to avoid disruption and may necessitate temporary staffing backfill.
review: This lever manages capital allocation and operational risk during a resource-constrained pilot phase. The options overlook renovation timeline variability and do not specify which metrics (data quality, event yield, safety incidents) should trigger or halt expansion.

name: Data Sharing and Privacy Governance Model
consequences: Restricting bedroom video to on-site analysis and sharing only de-identified physiological/sensor data under data use agreements maximizes participant trust and regulatory compliance, but limits external algorithm validation and slows reproducibility. Depositing raw EEG, actigraphy, and annotated event logs in a repository (e.g., OpenNeuro with institutional IRB data-sharing addendum) enables secondary analysis and algorithm benchmarking by external researchers, accelerating science but requiring robust de-identification and consent procedures.
options: Share de-identified EEG, contact-free sensor data (mattress, infrared movement, ambient audio post-processing), and event annotations via a controlled-access repository (e.g., Zenodo restricted access), requiring external researchers to execute a data use agreement and acknowledge privacy constraints; retain video on secure local server for internal validation only.

Deposit anonymized physiological and event-timing data in an open public repository (OpenNeuro, Figshare) under CC-BY-4.0, reserving high-resolution video for a separate controlled-access institutional archive; this maximizes reproducibility but requires explicit consent expansion and careful de-identification workflows.

Establish a consortium-based federated analysis model: maintain all data (including video) locally, grant external collaborators remote query-only access to pre-computed summary statistics and model validation endpoints via a secure portal, avoiding data export while enabling external validation and collaboration.
review: This lever reconciles open science principles with privacy obligations inherent in residential monitoring. The options do not address the cost and effort required for consent expansion, de-identification validation, or management of consortium access infrastructure.

name: Pharmaceutical Partnership and Funding Contingency Model
consequences: Positioning pharma collaboration as optional upside (not a feasibility requirement) with retained academic publication rights and a 30-day pre-publication comment window preserves scientific independence and avoids false dependency on uncommitted funding. However, foregoing pharma partnership limits year-three budget flexibility (€800K conditional on month-24 progress) and may constrain secondary analysis aims if event-yield targets require extended follow-up beyond month 36.
options: Decline pharma partnerships in phase one; pursue phase-two expansion funding through a second DFG grant application (submitted by month 30) and University of Bonn internal competitive funding, accepting longer intervals between funding awards and potential operational hiatus.

Negotiate a non-binding feasibility-stage partnership with a sleep-disorder-focused pharma company (e.g., Idorsia, Takeda) to co-sponsor a subset of 10–15 participants for dedicated neurophysiological phenotyping and medication-challenge substudies, with contractual assurance of publication independence and no data exclusivity.

Diversify funding: solicit foundations focused on neurological research (e.g., Volkswagen Foundation) for expansion funding by month 20, submit a co-branded pharma-sponsored grant to the German Sleep Society, and retain internal university funding as baseline to eliminate single-source dependency.
review: This lever balances financial stability against scientific autonomy and funder diversity. The options overlook the time and effort required to identify and negotiate with appropriate pharma partners, or the hidden costs of managing multiple funding relationships.

name: Inclusion Criteria Stringency and Participant Preselection Depth
consequences: Stricter pre-admission screening (e.g., mandatory overnight screening polysomnography or extended phone-based behavioral assessment) raises confidence in parasomnia diagnosis and improves event capture rate within the residential window, reducing unproductive admissions. However, tighter criteria shrink the recruitment pool and may delay enrollment, pushing the timeline toward external review triggers and increasing per-participant acquisition costs.
options: Require all candidates to complete a standardized overnight screening PSG at University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic prior to residential admission, using PSG-confirmed arousal patterns and self-reported sleep architecture to stratify event probability and inform stay duration upfront.

Implement a structured 4-week pre-residential observational period using mailed dry-electrode EEG headbands and contact-free mattress sensors in participants' home environments, scoring baseline nocturnal movement to identify high-likelihood candidates and exclude mimics or extremely low-frequency sleepers before committing residential time.

Maintain current pre-admission pathway (clinical interview, collateral history, prior polysomnography) but add a mandatory 30-minute behavioral video interview with the clinical psychologist assessing sleep-related injury risk, medication history, and psychiatric stability, documented for reproducibility and early exclusion of borderline cases.
review: This lever directly governs the trade-off between enrollment velocity and event yield quality, yet the options differ substantially in cost and feasibility. Requiring screening PSG adds expense and delays enrollment by weeks, while home monitoring reduces cost but introduces inter-individual sensor variability and prolongs screening; neither approach addresses how stricter gates will be communicated to referral networks to maintain recruitment momentum.

name: Annotation Bottleneck Mitigation and Scoring Workflow Efficiency
consequences: Annotating nightly sensor streams and flagging probable events in real time, then re-scoring offline with dual raters, is labor-intensive and risks publication delays if inter-rater disagreements pile up. How annotation is staged and reviewed directly impacts technician workload, publication timeline, and the feasibility of the semi-automated event-triage algorithm.
options: Introduce a two-tier offline scoring protocol in which the night technician's preliminary annotations are reviewed by a single independent rater first; only events with disagreement or borderline morphology are escalated to dual-rater consensus, reducing total scoring hours while preserving reliability thresholds.

Establish a weekly live scoring session where the night technician, clinical psychologist, and one postdoctoral researcher review all flagged events from the preceding week together using a standardized consensus rubric, replacing asynchronous dual scoring and enabling real-time clarification of AASM criteria ambiguities.

Delegate overnight annotation to research technicians only for obvious sensor artifacts and clear movement events; reserve all subjective event borderline calls for a dedicated part-time sleep technician hired mid-study who specializes in AASM scoring and performs batch re-annotation of accumulated uncertain cases monthly rather than nightly.
review: This lever addresses labor allocation and reviewer consensus, but the options risk either over-filtering genuine events in the first approach or introducing synchronous bottlenecks in the second. None of the options explicitly handle how inter-rater disagreements on borderline parasomnia morphology will be arbitrated or how the semi-automated tool will be trained if human ground truth remains heterogeneous.

name: Technician Workload and Night-to-Day Transition Protocol
consequences: Eight active suites require coordinated technician presence for safety, real-time event flagging, and immediate PSG escalation during behavioral incidents. Staffing three rotating night technicians covers 8 suites only with narrow safety margin; workload imbalance or fatigue compromises incident detection and annotation quality, directly affecting event yield and data quality.
options: Cross-train all night technicians to conduct full PSG setup and scalp electrode placement during escalation events, eliminating the need for daytime callbacks and enabling immediate high-resolution capture of recurrent episodes within the same night while participants remain behaviorally activated.

Hire a fourth technician to staff a flexible rotating night schedule (2–3 nights per week) whose primary responsibility is real-time video review and event flagging, allowing the core three technicians to focus exclusively on PSG operations and annotation, creating clear workload separation.

Implement a duty-roster system where one technician per night is designated the event-response lead with authority to wake a second technician if a flagged event exceeds predefined severity thresholds (e.g., sustained violent movement, fall risk), with hourly handover documentation and monthly debriefs to surface workload imbalances and skill gaps.
review: This lever mitigates technician fatigue and incident response delays, but cross-training all technicians in PSG placement increases training burden upfront and may not be feasible if some staff lack EEG technical competency. The options also do not address how to handle simultaneous behavioral events in non-adjacent suites or whether daytime oversight of post-escalation participants is adequate.

name: Event Rarity and Statistical Power Management
consequences: The assumption that 70–80% of admitted participants will yield at least one captured event depends on reliable triggering of home-like conditions and accurate pre-admission event frequency reporting; if actual capture falls below 50%, the program risks missing the prespecified event threshold by month 18, triggering external review. Extending stays beyond the initial 2 weeks increases costs and participant burden, while tightening inclusion criteria to higher-frequency sleepers shrinks the recruitment pool.
options: Establish a mid-stay adaptive protocol: after 7 days residential, if no events have been captured, offer participants an optional stay extension to 4 weeks with contingent additional compensation (€40 per additional week) and intensified sleep-curtailing conditions (slightly earlier lights-out, minimal daytime napping) that may provoke parasomnia without compromising safety.

Enroll a secondary cohort of participants with lower event frequency (≥1 episode per month, versus ≥2 per month for the primary cohort) exclusively for baseline nocturnal physiology and sensor-activity characterization, reducing event-yield pressure while still building the matched control dataset for the semi-automated tool.

Design the residential stay as a paired protocol: all participants undergo an initial 2-week standard protocol, then return for a second 1-week intensive stay 2–3 months later with identical monitoring, allowing longitudinal clustering and variability patterns to emerge and increasing total events captured per individual without prolonging any single admission.
review: This lever acknowledges the statistical reality of rare events, but the options introduce distinct operational complexities. Adaptive extensions risk participant fatigue and safety drift if sleep curtailment is applied, secondary low-frequency cohorts require separate inclusion criteria and analysis, and paired return stays depend on participant retention and willingness to travel twice to Bonn.

name: Semi-Automated Event Triage Tool Development and Validation Scope
consequences: Building a triage model that ranks probable parasomnia episodes reduces manual review burden and enables faster event prioritization, but validation against dual human scorer agreement demands substantial ground-truth annotation upfront, and the model's sensitivity and specificity will depend critically on the heterogeneity and inter-rater agreement of the training data. Scope ambition directly impacts the timeline for Aim 3 completion and publication.
options: Scope the semi-automated tool as a binary classifier that distinguishes probable parasomnia events from non-event nocturnal movement and sensor noise using shallow feature engineering (movement magnitude, EMG energy, arousal timing) without deep learning, enabling validation on the first 100–150 captured events and publication of a methods paper by month 24.

Develop the triage tool as a multi-class ranking system that assigns each flagged event a likelihood score across four categories (high-confidence parasomnia, borderline parasomnia, non-parasomnia arousal, sensor artifact), requiring concurrent training of a secondary inter-rater agreement model to quantify confidence thresholds and deferring final validation until 200+ events are annotated.

Implement a lightweight real-time flagging algorithm during months 1–18 using simple heuristic thresholds (e.g., sustained EMG burst + movement intensity above 75th percentile) to reduce technician annotation burden, then retrofit the algorithm retrospectively with machine-learning calibration once ground-truth data accumulates, enabling phased tool maturation without delaying early publications.
review: This lever governs the ambition and timeline of Aim 3, but the options reflect different risk-reward profiles. The first approach prioritizes publication speed but may oversimplify parasomnia heterogeneity; the second offers finer discrimination but defers impact; the third balances immediate operational utility with eventual sophistication but risks technical debt if the heuristics prove inconsistent with downstream learning.

name: Facility Continuity and Operational Contingency Under Staffing or Safety Disruption
consequences: Residential monitoring depends on continuous night technician coverage and rapid clinical decision-making; unplanned technician absence, equipment failure, or a serious safety incident can force temporary closure, disrupting enrollment and data collection momentum. Contingency planning determines whether the program can absorb disruptions or whether a single event triggers cascading delays.
options: Maintain a standby technician employed at 0.3 FTE (on-call) available within 30 minutes of the facility; this person covers unexpected absences, provides surge capacity during participant clusters, and undertakes off-hours data backup and equipment maintenance, reducing single-point-of-failure risk.

Establish a tiered reduction protocol whereby if active suite occupancy falls below 4 participants due to staffing loss, the program temporarily scales to 4-suite operation with heightened safety monitoring and suspended new admissions until full staffing is restored, enabling continuity without compromising incident response.

Negotiate a reciprocal backup agreement with the University Hospital Bonn Sleep Clinic's night technician team whereby trained clinic staff can be mobilized to cover facility nights for up to 2 weeks in the event of illness or emergency departure of core technicians, in exchange for the facility providing EEG-BIDS data formatting training and access to research-grade equipment.
review: This lever directly mitigates operational fragility, but each option introduces distinct costs or dependencies. The standby technician adds 0.3 FTE salary and training overhead; suite reduction preserves staffing resilience but delays enrollments and may breach month-18 engagement targets; the backup agreement is low-cost but depends on external partner willingness and may distract clinic staff from clinical duties.

name: Pharmaceutical Partnership Terms and Secondary Funding Leverage
consequences: A pharma partnership can offset participant compensation, algorithm development, and publication costs if structured to preserve academic independence. However, vague partnership terms risk scope creep (e.g., proprietary algorithm ownership, extended pre-publication embargo, unstated commercial expectations), which may delay publications, complicate data sharing, and erode the team's focus on the three core scientific aims.
options: Negotiate a limited pharma partnership in which the partner funds €300–500K of year-two and year-three operations (participant compensation, equipment upgrades, independent scoring contractor) in exchange for named acknowledgment, a 30-day pre-publication review window, and rights to fund a separate post-hoc exploratory analysis of their compound in a subset of collected data, keeping the primary aims and dataset fully in the academic domain.

Decline direct pharma funding and instead establish a formal equipment-loan agreement whereby a pharma partner provides advanced portable PSG systems or home monitoring devices at zero cost; the company receives a single methodology acknowledgment and case-study presentation at an annual conference, but no data rights or publication approval authority.

Reserve pharmaceutical engagement strictly for the phase-two grant proposal stage, ensuring the residential facility and Aims 1–3 are fully completed and published under pure academic funding before introducing industry collaboration, reducing near-term financial pressure but accepting slower year-one infrastructure build.
review: This lever addresses financial resilience versus academic autonomy, but the options differ in timing and transparency risk. The first approach unlocks significant co-funding but requires careful vetting of the pharma partner's research interests and publication intentions; the second avoids funding entanglement but may not materialize if no partner finds the equipment loan attractive; the third preserves independence but increases dependency on DFG and university funding sufficiency.

name: Baseline Environmental Sensor Calibration and Artifact Filtering
consequences: Dry-electrode EEG headbands and contact-free mattress sensors generate high-noise data in home-like settings; aggressive filtering removes true micro-arousals but loose filtering floods the annotation queue with movement artifacts and environmental noise. Choosing a calibration strategy determines whether the night technician can reliably pre-screen events in real time or must defer all decisions to dual-scorer review, directly affecting workload distribution and event-capture latency.
options: Conduct a 2-week in-house calibration phase on the first 3–5 control participants before opening full enrollment, mapping sensor artifact signatures (mattress motion, AC hum, door creaks) to establish facility-specific thresholds for each sensor stream that balance sensitivity against false-positive suppression.

Partner with the dry-electrode headband and mattress-sensor manufacturers to apply their standard laboratory calibration templates directly to the facility's ambient conditions, accepting manufacturer-recommended filtering without facility-specific tuning and relying on downstream dual scoring to catch missed events.

Implement a hybrid calibration where low-cost movement and environmental sensors (microphone, temperature, humidity, light) are field-calibrated in-house while EEG and EMG signal processing relies on published artifact-detection algorithms from prior residential sleep studies, minimizing calibration overhead but potentially accumulating residual noise in the annotation queue.
review: This lever determines whether the facility operates in a low-latency, technician-driven mode or a high-latency, dual-scorer-dependent mode, but none of the options address the cost and timeline of involving manufacturers in calibration support or the risk that published algorithms may not transfer to the Bonn facility's specific acoustic and electrical environment.

name: Dual-Scorer Workload Smoothing and Annotation Sequencing
consequences: Two independent raters must score every captured event and night against AASM criteria; sequencing affects when disagreement is detected and resolved. Early-pipeline disagreement (e.g., flagging conflicting AASM-criterion interpretations within 48 hours of capture) allows protocol corrections before the participant leaves, while post-hoc consensus-building after discharge delays publication and requires callback for clarification if phenotyping hinges on borderline events. Workload also depends on how many raw segments reach the scorers—loose real-time filtering overwhelms scorers, tight filtering risks missing true events.
options: Require both scorers to independently rate all full-montage nights and escalation-flagged events in real time during the participant's residential stay, with PI-mediated consensus resolution within 24 hours of stay completion, enabling rapid protocol adjustment and participant callback if needed, but concentrating scorer workload and potentially delaying other study phases.

Batch event scoring by participant and time window, performing dual scoring in weekly blocks after the participant exits the facility, allowing scorer flexibility and reduced context switching, but introducing a 1–2 week lag before disagreements are identified and complicating any participant-specific protocol adjustments.

Implement a three-tier scoring workflow where the night technician flags probable events at capture using facility-specific templates, a single dedicated scorer reviews all flagged segments within 48 hours and documents confidence ratings, and dual independent scoring occurs only for segments scoring below a prespecified confidence threshold or flagged for inter-rater resolution, reducing baseline scorer burden while preserving rigor on uncertain cases.
review: This lever controls the pace and distribution of scoring labor, directly impacting publication timeline and data quality assurance, yet the options do not address how to train both scorers to maintain consistent AASM-criterion interpretation across weeks of independent work or how to handle rater drift as the study progresses.

name: Participant Stay Duration Flexibility and Extended-Admission Protocols
consequences: Current policy allows initial 2–8 week stays with optional 2-week extension (max 10 weeks) if zero events are captured, capped at 20% unproductive admissions. Shorter stays reduce per-participant operational cost and accelerate turnover, but low-yield participants risk exclusion before a true event occurs, potentially biasing the final cohort toward high-frequency episodiers. Longer stays capture more events per participant but strain facility occupancy, staff fatigue, and participant compliance.
options: Adopt a graduated extension policy where participants with at least one captured event proceed on schedule, while participants with zero events automatically receive a 1-week extension at no additional participant cost and only if facility occupancy permits, with extension duration tied to ongoing facility census rather than a fixed 2-week cap, balancing event yield against operational predictability.

Establish a pre-admission event-yield screening step using 1-week outpatient actigraphy and bed partner interview, admitting only participants with high actigraphy-derived sleep fragmentation or frequent reported collateral episodes, accepting recruitment delay and pre-selection bias in exchange for higher in-facility event capture rates and shorter average stays.

Maintain the current fixed 2–8 week plus optional 2-week extension protocol without modification, accepting that 20% of admissions may yield zero events and treating unproductive stays as a necessary cost of capturing rare behavior in an unselected population, with the trade-off that publication timelines are longer and event-triage algorithm training data is sparser initially.
review: This lever directly trades enrollment diversity against event yield and operational stability, but the options do not account for how participant motivation and compliance decay during extended stays or how multi-week admissions affect sleep debt and masking of baseline parasomnia frequency.

name: Control Group Recruitment Timing and Matching Depth
consequences: Matched control participants provide baseline nocturnal movement and arousal patterns to benchmark the event-triage tool against normal sleep; recruiting controls requires separate inclusion/exclusion criteria and runs in parallel with parasomnia enrollment. Tight matching on age, sex, and baseline sleep fragmentation strengthens the control comparison but slows recruitment and increases screening burden. Loose matching or delayed control recruitment simplifies logistics but weakens the tool-validation comparison and defers publication of benchmarking results.
options: Concurrently recruit control participants on a 1:1 ratio with parasomnia enrollees, matched on age band and sex, with sleep-fragmentation matching deferred to post-hoc statistical adjustment in the analysis phase, balancing recruitment parallelism against screening complexity and enabling comparative analyses by mid-year two.

Defer all control recruitment to year two after parasomnia enrollment stabilizes, then recruit a single larger cohort of controls in an expedited track, accepting that control data collection lags parasomnia data collection by 6–12 months but allowing recruitment expertise and sensor-setup workflows to mature first and reducing concurrent staffing strain.

Recruit a smaller convenience control cohort from University Hospital Bonn staff and local sleep-medicine professionals, matched only on available demographic data and known sleep health, accepting reduced comparability in exchange for faster recruitment and allowing resource-intensive 1:1 matching to be reserved for a future expansion phase.
review: This lever determines the statistical power and credibility of the event-triage tool's benchmarking claims, but the options overlook how site-specific sensor artifact patterns and environmental noise in the residential facility may differ significantly from controls' home sleep patterns even after matching.

name: Semi-Automated Triage Tool Development Pace and Validation Stringency
consequences: Aim 3 requires developing a tool that ranks probable parasomnia episodes to reduce manual review burden; validation is benchmarked against dual human scoring, not autonomous diagnosis. A rapid-iteration approach deploys early versions by month 12 to guide real-time technician flagging, accelerating event capture but risking over-reliance on incomplete models. A conservative approach reserves tool development until year two after sufficient labeled event data accumulates, ensuring higher-quality training data but delaying tool deployment and extending manual annotation workload through year one.
options: Begin semi-automated tool development in parallel with month-3 enrollment on a rolling basis using early captured events, releasing an alpha version by month 12 for in-house technician guidance only (not clinical decision-making), then validate against dual scoring retrospectively, accepting high false-positive rates in early versions but building iterative feedback into the algorithm and shortening the path to a stable tool by year two.

Delay formal tool development until month 18 when at least 40 independently adjudicated events and a mature control dataset are available, then conduct a focused 6-month development sprint using high-confidence labeled data, ensuring the validation dataset is clean and the final model generalizes well, but deferring tool-assisted event flagging and extending human annotation burden through much of year two.

Adopt a hybrid approach where a rule-based preliminary screening tool (e.g., simple thresholds on EEG delta power, movement amplitude, and arousal-associated microstructure from published literature) is deployed from month 3 for technician pre-filtering, while a machine-learning model development begins in month 9 in parallel with ongoing scoring, allowing rapid filtering gains upfront while building the training dataset for a more sophisticated model by month 18.
review: This lever governs the risk-return profile of algorithm maturity versus annotation efficiency, yet none of the options explicitly address how to prevent the tool's biases (e.g., favoring high-amplitude movement) from systematically mislabeling quieter parasomnia subtypes or how to validate the tool's transfer to future facilities with different sensor and environmental configurations.

name: Inter-Rater Reliability Targets and Disagreement Resolution Process
consequences: AASM-criterion scoring involves subjective judgment on arousal definitions, muscle-atonia boundaries, and event morphology; inter-rater agreement targets determine acceptable divergence thresholds and when escalation to the PI for consensus is required. High agreement targets (e.g., Cohen's kappa > 0.80 on all event classes) enforce tight standardization but slow scoring and consume PI time on edge cases. Looser targets (e.g., kappa > 0.60) accelerate scoring but risk publishing results with low internal consistency and weaker claims about event phenotyping.
options: Establish a tiered agreement target where common, unambiguous events (e.g., full-tonic confusional arousals) must reach kappa > 0.75, ambiguous boundary cases (e.g., micro-arousal + minimal movement) are accepted at kappa > 0.60 with rater-specific confidence notes, and systematic disagreement patterns trigger PI-led retraining, balancing rigor against practical workflow and documenting where scoring uncertainty exists.

Set a single cohort-wide inter-rater agreement floor of kappa > 0.70 for all event classes, with all disagreements escalated to a third-party adjudicator (a senior sleep-medicine neurologist external to the primary scoring team) who casts the tiebreaker, ensuring conservative quality control but adding cost and delaying event finalization by 1–2 weeks per batch.

Require kappa > 0.80 for all event types without exception, with disagreements triggering PI-led joint re-review, shared reference-literature study, and re-scoring of the entire prior batch if systematic bias is detected, maintaining publication-grade rigor but potentially extending the scoring timeline by 30–50% and straining the PI's direct involvement in annotation.
review: This lever controls the credibility of Aim 2 phenotyping claims and publication standards, but the options do not address how rater training decay over a 3-year study affects baseline agreement or how to benchmark inter-rater reliability meaningfully when event subtypes (e.g., RBD) may naturally yield lower agreement due to clinical heterogeneity.

name: Facility Contingency Surge Capacity and Participant Safety Escalation Pathways
consequences: The facility operates 8 active suites with rotation of 3 night technicians; unexpected staff illness, participant safety incidents, or equipment failure can reduce effective capacity. Redundancy-planning determines response latency and whether admissions must pause or slow during disruptions. Over-investing in surge capacity (e.g., 4 technicians, equipment redundancy) protects continuity but strains the year-one budget; under-investing risks operational collapse and data loss if multiple single-points-of-failure coincide.
options: Hire a 4th dedicated night technician scheduled 2–3 nights per week as an on-call resource and concurrent backup for the primary rotation, maintaining flexible weekend coverage and a buffer against short-term absences, with the trade-off that year-one personnel costs increase approximately €60–80K and the role transitions to shared annotation or data-engineering work if external surge does not materialize.

Establish a protocol requiring immediate admission pause if more than one primary technician is unavailable simultaneously, with a 48-hour grace period to recover staffing before reopening; simultaneously negotiate reciprocal on-call sleep-technician support with University Hospital Bonn's main sleep center to provide emergency coverage for no more than 1–2 nights in a true crisis, reducing surge costs but accepting that single-technician absence still reduces monitored suites from 8 to 4–5 and accelerates participant throughput unpredictably.

Partner with a regional staffing agency to maintain a pool of pre-trained sleep-technician contractors available at 24–48 hour notice, bundling contractor training into the month-1 facility setup, bearing incremental agency fees (estimated €15–20 per hour above standard technician salary) but deferring large fixed salaries and allowing fluid capacity adjustment without long-term commitment.
review: This lever mitigates operational fragility and protects the study timeline against foreseeable disruptions, but the options do not address how to rapidly onboard and quality-assure substitute technicians in real time, especially if they lack prior exposure to the facility's specific sensor systems and AASM-scoring culture, potentially introducing annotation drift.

name: Publication Strategy and Interim-Results Disclosure Cadence
consequences: Success criteria require 2–3 peer-reviewed publications by month 36; a rapid-fire strategy publishes methods-validation and early phenotyping results by month 24, accelerating dissemination but risking over-interpretation of sparse data. A consolidated strategy defers publication until year three, gathering fuller event counts and more robust statistics but delaying scientific impact and jeopardizing grant-renewal narratives. Interim disclosure (e.g., conference abstracts, preprints) bridges the gap but carries reputational risk if early findings are later contradicted.
options: Target a methods-validation manuscript by month 18 (facility safety, sensor accuracy, technician-flagging reliability) and a longitudinal-phenotyping preprint by month 24 submitted to a sleep-medicine journal, followed by a formal peer-reviewed publication by month 30, ensuring early visibility and allowing feedback incorporation while preserving full data integrity by final submission.

Withhold peer-reviewed publication until month 28–32, concentrating on a single high-impact journal submission with full 50–70 participant cohort and complete event-triage algorithm validation, maximizing statistical power and narrative coherence but accepting visibility lag and the risk that a competing group publishes similar results first.

Publish a rapid methods-validation letter or brief communication by month 15–18 in a open-access journal, present preliminary phenotyping findings at major sleep conferences (APSS, ESRS) by month 18–22, and defer full journal manuscripts until month 30–36, balancing early dissemination against publication fragmentation and ensuring continuous funding-renewal messaging without overdrafting credibility on incomplete data.
review: This lever shapes the project's scientific visibility and career trajectories of the team, but the options do not address how to handle potential secondary analyses by external collaborators using the shared dataset or how to manage attribution and data-use agreements if early findings catalyze unexpected follow-up work.

name: Pharmaceutical Partner Engagement Scope and Intellectual-Property Boundaries
consequences: Pharmaceutical partnership is optional upside funding, not a feasibility requirement; the partnership agreement grants 30-day pre-publication comment rights only, with the academic team retaining full publication authority and data ownership. Closer pharma engagement (e.g., co-authorship, shared data access, early algorithm feedback) accelerates secondary analyses and provides additional funding but entangles publication timelines and risks creating perception of bias. Arm's-length engagement simplifies governance but may leave funding and algorithmic validation incomplete if the partner later demands tighter control.
options: If a pharma partner materializes, grant them (a) non-voting observation rights at semi-annual advisory board meetings, (b) access only to de-identified, non-video physiological data through a formal data-use agreement, and (c) the 30-day pre-publication comment window, explicitly excluding co-authorship and algorithm training-set access unless the partner funds a separate secondary-analysis track with its own ethics approval and publication timeline.

Negotiate a deeper partnership structure where the pharma partner co-funds Aim 3 algorithm development and receives algorithm-source-code access under patent-pending agreement, with a revenue-sharing clause on any future commercialization, in exchange for accepting delayed publication (up to 60 days for patent filing) and acknowledging the partner in methods-section funding disclosures, trading governance clarity against publication friction.

Actively solicit only research-support funding (equipment, technician salary top-ups, travel for collaborative meetings) from pharmaceutical partners without data access or algorithm involvement, keeping the dataset and publications entirely within the academic consortium, at the cost of reduced partner leverage and lower likelihood of sustained pharma funding beyond the initial grant.
review: This lever determines whether the project can leverage pharma investment for algorithm maturity without sacrificing academic independence, but the options do not clarify how to enforce publication embargo terms if a partner later claims urgent need for data access due to a regulatory filing or competing product launch.
